title: IV. PhIP-Seq: immunoprecipitation and phage amplification methods
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"dlu7f","text":"This protocol describes the use of the PanSeq T7-phage display library for the identification of antibody reactivity against phage displayed peptides. Biospecimens are combined with the phage library to allow for antibody-antigen complex formation followed by bead-based immunoprecipitation and subsequent phage amplification. Multiple rounds of immunoprecipitation result in enrichment for a subset of the phage library, which is then prepared for Next Generation Sequencing and computational analysis.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"6rokk","text":"Key materials and reagents have been listed under the \"Materials\" tab for user convenience to ensure reproducibility. All other materials and reagents may be sourced from reputable suppliers providing molecular-biology grade reagents. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"60h1i","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"8hiqq","text":"Appropriate Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) is recommended at all times. Frequent glove changes during phage work reduce the risk of contamination.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"5fk5u","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"1stlt","text":"The T7 bacteriophage is highly infectious to E. coli cultures. Therefore, the use of T7 bacteriophage must involve a high degree of attention to avoid the risk of surface contamination which may impact other E. coli based research in the lab. Fastidious and regular decontamination of work surfaces and instruments with a dilute-bleach solution (1 part bleach to 4 parts water) is essential to reducing contamination. Waste should be carefully packaged and removed from the lab space on a daily basis. The ideal environment for phage work would allow for complete isolation of phage related work in an isolated space away from all other bacterial work.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"c2s2a","text":"This protocol involves the use of microorganisms, which requires registration with the user's Institutional Biosafety Committee (IBC) prior to initiating experimental work.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: Prior to initiating a new PhIP-seq run, the PanSeq sublibraries of interest should be identified based on which part of the antibody repertoire is to be profiled. Ensure that a sufficient volume of phage library stock of known titer is on hand to complete the entire sample set. ( Note: The titer of the phage pools should be at least 1x10 11 pfu/mL.)

If more than one library pool will be used, the titers should be used to approximate a similar peptide coverage based on the library size (number of unique peptides) per pool. The pools should be combined in a sufficient volume to account for the total number of samples and controls in the cohort. The total volume of PanSeq library mix used for each sample is 100 μL, with the volume balance made up with 1% IgG-free BSA in TBS-T. A B C D E 1 Agilent ID Pool ID Length (bp) Size (n) Description 2 3470431 P1 230 244,000 Human 3 3470441 P2 230 244,000 Human 4 3470451 P3 230 244,000 Human 5 3470461 P4 230 211,385 Human 6 3470391 P5 230 244,000 VirScan2 + SARS-CoV-2 7 3470401 P6 230 238,226 VirScan2 + SARS-CoV-2 8 3470421 P7 230 244,000 Microbiome 9 3465521 P8 206 88,854 ToxScan 10 3467041 P9 230 32,131 Full IEDB Table 1. TIU PanSeq Oligo Pools Record Panseq pools used:

Start an overnight culture of BLT5403 cells in Terrific Broth containing carbenicillin (100 μg/mL) the day of the initial post-IP amplification (day two and thereafter). A 10 mL culture in a 50mL conical tube at 37°C and 250 rpm is sufficient for use as an inoculation culture.

The phage immunoprecipitations are performed in 96-well semi-skirted PCR plates. To reduce the potential for non-specific binding of the phage to the PCR plate, the plates are blocked overnight at 4°C with 3% IgG-free BSA in TBS-Tween20 (0.1%v/v) sealed with a type-F foil adhesive plate sealer.

Based on the sample type, prepare appropriate dilutions of serum and control antibodies. For biospecimens, CSF is used undiluted and human serum or plasma is diluted 1:20 into 1% IgG-free BSA in TBS-T with 0.02% sodium azide. Control antibodies for within-pool controls should be prepared to yield a final concentration of 10 μg/mL.
Completely remove the blocking solution from the PCR plate by firmly decanting the solution into a waste container and reverse spinning the plate.
Transfer 100 μL of the PanSeq phage pool aseptically to each well of the 96-well PCR plate.

Transfer 2 μL of diluted serum, plasma, or control monoclonal/polyclonal antibody to the designated wells in each PCR plate. For CSF samples, transfer 10 μL of undiluted CSF into the respective wells. Seal the PCR plate using the type-F foil adhesive sealer, being certain to thoroughly seal every well to avoid cross-contamination. Figure 1: Exemplar PhIP-seq plate layout . Samples are generally performed in technical replicates in adjacent wells, for up to 40 samples per plate. The remaining two columns are reserved for control monoclonal antibodies (C1-C6) and bead controls (B1,B2).
Invert the PCR plate several times to mix and then briefly spin in a plate centrifuge to collect the solution at the bottom of each well.
Allow the formation of phage immune-complexes to proceed at 4°C overnight (i.e. incubate). Optionally, place the PCR plate on an end-over-end rotating platform to enhance mixing.

The overnight phage immune-complexes are precipitated using protein A/G magnetic beads (ThermoFisher #88803). Note: The following steps should be performed early in the experimental day to ensure sufficient time to complete precipitation and subsequent amplification.

Prepare a fresh culture dilution of BLT5403 cells from the overnight culture in Terrific broth. A volume of 50mL of culture is required for each 96 deep-well plate of samples. Note : We have observed that phage enrichment is improved when using square-well deep plates. (Axygen #P-2ML-SQ-C-S)

Reconstitute A/G beads by gentle inversion until a homogenous suspension is formed. Transfer 10 μL of bead suspension for every sample (or ~1 mL per plate) into a sterile low-binding microcentrifuge tube. Depending on the number of samples being processed, use one tube per plate. Place the tube(s) onto a Dynabead-compatible magnet stand and allow beads to collect for 3 minutes. Record A/G bead lot#:
Prepare fresh blocking solution (3% IgG-free BSA/TBST) for protein A/G magnetic beads.

Refer to Step 4, prepare the required number of blocked PCR plates to perform the second round of immune-complex formation. Note : if this is the second round of immunoprecipitation you can disregard this step as there is no third round of immunoprecipitation.
Carefully aspirate the supernatant and then remove the tubes from the magnetic stand. Add 1 mL of blocking buffer to each tube and place onto a rotating mixer for 1 hour at room temperature.
Transfer 10 μL of blocked protein A/G beads to each well of the 96-well plate containing the phage immune-complexes. Place the plate on the shaker at 4°C for 1 hour.
Briefly spin the plates at ~1000 RCF to reduce cross-contamination risk. Place the plate on a 96-well magnetic plate (Permagen P/N S500) and allow beads to collect for 3 minutes.

Aspirate the supernatant from all wells and add 150 μL Tris-NP40 wash buffer (10 mM Tris HCl, pH 7.4, 140 mM NaCl, 0.1%(v/v) NP-40) per well. Note : The use of a higher stringency wash buffer may be substituted to reduce non-specific binding, yet this may compromise the specific signal depending on antibody affinity.
Place the plate back onto the magnet for 3 minutes and repeat as below for a total of 5 washes.
During incubation time during the washes, aliquot 500 μL of the BLT5403 culture into a 96-well deep block (2mL capacity/well) and place a breathable tape seal on the blocks.

Following the final aspiration of Tris-NP40 buffer from the protein A/G beads, gently resuspend the beads with 100 μL Terrific Broth and transfer to the corresponding wells of the 96-well deep block. Note: Use extreme care when transferring the bead-media mixture to the block to avoid cross-contamination.

Place the 96-well deep block(s) in the 37°C incubator on a microplate shaker (stroke radius 3mm) set to 700 RPM. Allow phage amplification to proceed for at least 3 hours; visually confirm lysis of the BLT5403 cells.
Pipet 60 μL (1/10th culture volume) of 5 M sodium chloride to each well. Incubate for 10 minutes at 37°C on a microplate shaker set to 700 RPM to ensure complete mixing and final lysis.
Centrifuge the block(s) at 3000x RCF for 30 min at 4°C to pellet cellular debris.

Transfer 100 μL of amplified phage to a freshly blocked plate and perform the second round of immunoprecipitation according to the steps outlined above. Note : The first amplification step can be saved at 4°C for a short period. Alternatively, cryopreserve the phage ( https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168170222003264 ) at -80°C. Retaining the phage amplifications from each round of immunoprecipitation is ideal in the event the user needs to repeat prior rounds of selection.
Transfer 25 μL of the amplified phage to a 96-well PCR plate containing 75 μL of PCR-grade water.
Heat-inactivate the diluted phage by incubation at 70°C for 15 minutes using a thermocycler.
Immediately use the heat-inactivated phage for barcoding PCR using phased primers (see associated protocol) or freeze the plates at -80°C for future processing.

The first of two PCR amplifications is performed to introduce phasing and template for priming of NGS barcodes and adapter sequence primers. Prepare 10 μM pools of the phasing primers for the first amplification of the heat-inactivated phage. A B 1 primer ID sequence 2 TEV_XGEN_0N-F ACACTCTTTCCCTACACGACGCTCTTCCGATCTCCTATACTTCCAAGGCGCA 3 TEV_XGEN_1N-F ACACTCTTTCCCTACACGACGCTCTTCCGATCTNCCTATACTTCCAAGGCGCA 4 TEV_XGEN_2N-F ACACTCTTTCCCTACACGACGCTCTTCCGATCTNNCCTATACTTCCAAGGCGCA 5 TEV_XGEN_3N-F ACACTCTTTCCCTACACGACGCTCTTCCGATCTNNNCCTATACTTCCAAGGCGCA 6 CP1_XGEN_0N-R GACTGGAGTTCAGACGTGTGCTCTTCCGATCTGCCAAGACAGAGAGTCACC 7 CP1_XGEN_1N-R GACTGGAGTTCAGACGTGTGCTCTTCCGATCTNGCCAAGACAGAGAGTCACC 8 CP1_XGEN_2N-R GACTGGAGTTCAGACGTGTGCTCTTCCGATCTNNGCCAAGACAGAGAGTCACC 9 CP1_XGEN_3N-R GACTGGAGTTCAGACGTGTGCTCTTCCGATCTNNNGCCAAGACAGAGAGTCACC Table 2. Dephasing primer sequences Figure 2 . Graphic example of phasing primers from NGI Sweden . Phase primers differ by purple nucleotides, which shift identical sequences relative to each other. Thus, they are "out of phase." Phase primers artificially increase library complexity, thereby increasing the accuracy of sequencing reads.

Setup the first PCR amplification reaction of the heat-inactivated phage using the above primer set as follows: A B 1 component volume per reaction (μL) 2 heat-inactivated phage 1 3 5x Q5 Reaction Buffer 5 4 10 μM Dephasing Primer Mix 1 5 10mM dNTP mix 0.5 6 Q5 DNA Polymerase 0.2 7 PCR-grade water 17.3 8 Total volume 25 Table 3. Amplification reaction setup Note : Prepare a scaled master mix for the dephasing PCR according to the total number of samples.

Perform the dephasing amplification of the heat-inactivated phage samples using the following thermal cycler temperature parameters: A B C 1 initial denaturation: 98°C 2min 2 13-16 cycles: 98°C 15s 3 68°C 15s 4 72°C 30s 5 Final extension: 72°C 2min 6 Final hold: 10°C HOLD Table 4. Thermal cycler settings Note : The amplification cycle number may require empirical determination when using samples with lower antibody concentrations, such as CSF. We have found that 13 cycles is sufficient for serum and monoclonal samples, and 16 cycles is sufficient for CSF samples.
Following the primary amplification, dilute the reactions 1:10 using PCR-grade water and proceed to the second PCR to append NGS compatible barcode sequences.

The phasing primers used in the first amplification are compatible with the xGEN UDI 10 nt primer sets. Set up the second PCR for barcoding as follows: A B 1 component volume per reaction (μL) 2 dilute dephasing PCR (1:10) 1 3 5x Q5 Reaction Buffer 5 4 xGEN UDI 10 nt primer set 1 5 10 mM dNTP mix 0.5 6 Q5 DNA Polymerase 0.2 7 PCR-grade water 17.3 8 Total Volume 25 Table 5. Amplification reaction setup Barcoding primer set plate ID(s)

Perform the barcoding amplification of the dilute dephasing PCR samples using the following thermal cycler temperature parameters: A B C 1 initial denaturation: 98°C 2min 2 6 cycles: 98°C 15s 3 68°C 15s 4 72°C 30s 5 Final extension: 72°C 2min 6 Final hold: 10°C HOLD Table 6. Thermal cycler settings
Perform Ampure XP bead-based isolation of the resulting barcoded library. For further reagent information, refer to the Ampure XP manual under the Description section.
Determine the concentration of each amplified dsDNA library pool using the Qubit high-sensitivity dsDNA quantification kit. Dilute each library to a concentration of 20 ng/μL using PCR-grade water.

Figure 3 . Assess the quality of the amplified dsDNA library pool by gel electrophoresis or preferably a high resolution analytical method such as Agilent TapeStation. A single amplicon of the predicted size should be observed and absent of primer dimer artifacts. Figure 3. Exemplar TapeStation data following NGS barcoding and Ampure XP bead purification

Normalize the total mass for each sample plate to create a final pool for submission to the NGS service of user's choice. For example, if the user is submitting a 50 μL sample at 20 ng/μL the total mass required is 1000 ng. If the user is submitting pools from four 96-well plates, then the total mass input from each 96-well plate pool should be 250 ng in a combined volume of 50 μL, with the volume balance coming from PCR-grade water.

Store the remaining phased and barcoded library at -20°C until NGS data has been assessed. This will allow for troubleshooting and/or prevent the need to repeat prior steps if NGS sequencing needs to be repeated.

Incubate the BLT5403 cells at 37°C and 250 RPM shaking in a baffled flask until the OD 600 reaches ~0.5. Proceed directly to phage amplification or store the culture at 4°C until the immunoprecipitation is complete.
Wash 2: Aspirate the supernatant from all wells and add 150 μL Tris-NP40 wash buffer (10 mM Tris HCl, pH 7.4, 140 mM NaCl, 0.1%(v/v) NP-40) per well.
Wash 3: Aspirate the supernatant from all wells and add 150 μL Tris-NP40 wash buffer (10 mM Tris HCl, pH 7.4, 140 mM NaCl, 0.1%(v/v) NP-40) per well.
Wash 4: Aspirate the supernatant from all wells and add 150 μL Tris-NP40 wash buffer (10 mM Tris HCl, pH 7.4, 140 mM NaCl, 0.1%(v/v) NP-40) per well.
Wash 5: Aspirate the supernatant from all wells and add 150 μL Tris-NP40 wash buffer (10 mM Tris HCl, pH 7.4, 140 mM NaCl, 0.1%(v/v) NP-40) per well.

For each barcoding PCR reaction plate, pool 10 μL from each well a single 2 mL microcentrifuge tube followed by the addition of 0.9x volume Ampure XP beads, thoroughly mix, and allow binding to occur for 10 mins.
Briefly spin the microcentrifuge tube in a low-speed table-top centrifuge.
Place the microcentrifuge tube on a compatible magnetic rack and allow the beads to pellet for 2-3 min. Carefully remove the supernatant into a waste container.
Gently wash the beads with 70% (v/v) ethanol twice, paying careful attention to the bead pellet to avoid disruption and loss.
After the removal of the second ethanol wash volume, allow the bead pellet to air dry for 5min. Note : avoid over-drying as this may reduce the recovery of the dsDNA.

Resuspend the pellet in 100 μL PCR-grade water, allowing the dsDNA to elute for 5min with gentle agitation, followed by magnetic pulldown of the pellet. Transfer the eluted dsDNA to a new microcentrifuge tube. Note : It was observed that a quantity of beads may slough away from the tube during transfer if the user attempts to recover the full volume. Therefore, we recommend only attempting to recover ~90% of the reconstitution volume.
creator: Thomas Esparza
